Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la public ardhi.

sections.title: Community land
sections.simplified: This says what land belongs to a community.
sections.swSimplified: Hii says nini ardhi ni ya a community.
sections.originalText:
(1) Community land shall vest in and be held by communities identified on the basis of ethnicity, culture or similar community of interest.
(2) Community land consists of— (a) land lawfully registered in the name of group representatives under the provisions of any law;
(b) land lawfully transferred to a specific community by any process of law;
(c) any other land declared to be community land by an Act of Parliament; and
(d) land that is— (i) lawfully held, managed or used by specific communities as community forests, grazing areas or shrines;
(ii) ancestral lands and lands traditionally occupied by hunter-gatherer communities; or
(iii) lawfully held as trust land by the county governments,

but not including any public land held in trust by the county government under Article 62(2).
(3) Any unregistered community land shall be held in trust by county governments on behalf of the communities for which it is held.
(4) Community land shall not be disposed of or otherwise used except in terms of legislation specifying the nature and extent of the rights of members of each community individually and collectively.
(5) Parliament shall enact legislation to give effect to this Article.
examples: Some land belongs to a community and should be held for that community, not taken by outsiders unfairly.
A community has always used or managed certain land. why that land matters to them.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ardhi ya jamii.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la ardhi ya jamii.

sections.title: Private land
sections.simplified: This says what land belongs to private owners.
sections.swSimplified: Hii says nini ardhi ni ya private owners.
examples: Some land belongs to private people or families.
A person owns land by title or lease. that it is private land.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ardhi ya kibinafsi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la ardhi ya kibinafsi.

sections.title: Landholding by non-citizens
sections.simplified: This says when a non-Kenyan can hold land.
sections.swSimplified: Hii says wakati a non-Mkenya inaweza hold ardhi.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person who is not a citizen may hold land on the basis of leasehold tenure only, and any such lease, however granted, shall not exceed ninety-nine years.
(2) If a provision of any agreement, deed, conveyance or document of whatever nature purports to confer on a person who is not a citizen an interest in land greater than a ninety-nine year lease, the provision shall be regarded as conferring on the person a ninety-nine year leasehold interest, and no more.
(3) For purposes of this Article— (a) a body corporate shall be regarded as a citizen only if the body corporate is wholly owned by one or more citizens; and
(b) property held in trust shall be regarded as being held by a citizen only if all of the beneficial interest of the trust is held by persons who are citizens.

(4) Parliament may enact legislation to make further provision for the operation of this Article.
examples: A person who is not Kenyan can only hold land by lease, not forever.
A non-citizen is given land for longer than the law allows. the limit.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu landholding by non-raia.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la landholding by non-raia.

sections.title: Regulation of land use and property
sections.swTitle: Udhibiti wa matumizi ya ardhi na mali
sections.simplified: Government can make rules on how land is used.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu udhibiti wa matumizi ya ardhi na mali.
sections.originalText:
(1) The State may regulate the use of any land, or any interest in or right over any land, in the interest of defence, public safety, public order, public morality, public health, or land use planning.
(2) Parliament shall enact legislation ensuring that investments in property benefit local communities and their economies.
examples: Government can make land-use rules for health, safety, planning, and the public good.
Rules can be made when a dangerous building, quarry, or project needs control.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu udhibiti wa matumizi ya ardhi na mali.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la udhibiti wa matumizi ya ardhi na mali.

sections.title: National Land Commission
sections.simplified: This sets up the land office.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu national Land Commission.
examples: The land office named here handles those matters.
Public land is being managed badly. why a land office exists.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu national Land Commission.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la national Land Commission.

sections.title: Legislation on land
sections.simplified: Parliament can make laws about land.
sections.swSimplified: Bunge inaweza kutunga sheria about ardhi.
examples: Parliament can make more laws to protect land rights and fix land problems.
Kenya needs clearer land rules on things like family land or land size. Parliament can make them.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu sheria kuhusu ardhi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la sheria kuhusu ardhi.

sections.title: Obligations in respect of the environment
sections.simplified: Everyone deserves a clean and healthy environment.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu wajibu kuhusu mazingira.
examples: People and government should help protect the environment when a factory dirties a river or smoke makes an area unsafe.
Trees are being destroyed carelessly or land is being damaged. the environment must be looked after.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu wajibu kuhusu mazingira.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la wajibu kuhusu mazingira.

sections.title: Enforcement of environmental rights
sections.simplified: Everyone deserves a clean and healthy environment.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu utekelezaji wa haki za mazingira.
sections.originalText:
(1) If a person alleges that a right to a clean and healthy environment recognised and protected under Article 42 has been, is being or is likely to be, denied, violated, infringed or threatened, the person may apply to a court for redress in addition to any other legal remedies that are available in respect to the same matter.
(2) On application under clause (1), the court may make any order, or give any directions, it considers appropriate— (a) to prevent, stop or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment;
(b) to compel any public officer to take measures to prevent or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment; or
(c) to provide compensation for any victim of a violation of the right to a clean and healthy environment.

(3) For the purposes of this Article, an applicant does not have to demonstrate that any person has incurred loss or suffered injury.
examples: People can complain or go to court when a river, forest, or neighbourhood is being damaged.
Pollution is harming a community. citizens do not have to stay quiet.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu utekelezaji wa haki za mazingira.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la utekelezaji wa haki za mazingira.

sections.title: Agreements relating to natural resources
sections.simplified: Big resource deals should follow the Constitution.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu agreements relating to natural resources.
sections.originalText:
(1) A transaction is subject to ratification by Parliament if it— (a) involves the grant of a right or concession by or on behalf of any person, including the national government, to another person for the exploitation of any natural resource of Kenya; and
(b) is entered into on or after the effective date.

(2) Parliament shall enact legislation providing for the classes of transactions subject to ratification under clause (1).
examples: The deal should follow the Constitution when leaders want to sign a big oil, gas, or mining deal.
People should not be cheated by secret or unfair agreements on natural resources.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu agreements relating to natural resources.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la agreements relating to natural resources.

sections.title: Legislation relating to the environment
sections.simplified: Everyone deserves a clean and healthy environment.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sheria kuhusu mazingira.
sections.originalText: Parliament shall enact legislation to give full effect to the provisions of this Part.
examples: Parliament can make those laws when Kenya needs clearer rules on forests, pollution, or natural resources.
People want stronger environmental protection. laws can be passed to support it.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu sheria kuhusu mazingira.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la sheria kuhusu mazingira.

sections.title: Responsibilities of leadership
sections.simplified: A leader should serve people, not themselves.
sections.swSimplified: A kiongozi inapaswa serve watu, not themselves.
examples: A leader uses office to help themselves. that is wrong.
People expect honesty and fairness from leaders. why.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu majukumu ya uongozi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la majukumu ya uongozi.

sections.title: Oath of office of State officers
sections.swTitle: Oath of ofisi of maafisa wa Serikali
sections.simplified: State officers promise to serve honestly.
sections.swSimplified: Maafisa wa Serikali promise to serve kwa uaminifu.
sections.originalText: Before assuming a State office, acting in a State office, or performing any functions of a State office, a person shall take and subscribe to the oath or affirmation of office, in the manner and form prescribed by the Third Schedule or under an Act of Parliament.
examples: A State officer must promise to serve Kenya honestly before taking office.
A leader breaks the promise they made when taking office. that oath matters.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu oath of ofisi of maafisa wa Serikali.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la oath of ofisi of maafisa wa Serikali.

sections.title: Conduct of State officers
sections.simplified: State officers should behave honestly and fairly.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu maadili ya maafisa wa Serikali.
sections.originalText:
(1) A State officer shall behave, whether in public and official life, in private life, or in association with other persons, in a manner that avoids— (a) any conflict between personal interests and public or official duties;
(b) compromising any public or official interest in favour of a personal interest; or
(c) demeaning the office the officer holds.

(2) A person who contravenes clause (1), or Article 76, 77 or 78(2)— (a) shall be subject to the applicable disciplinary procedure for the relevant office; and
(b) may, in accordance with the disciplinary procedure referred to in paragraph (a), be dismissed or otherwise removed from office.

(3) A person who has been dismissed or otherwise removed from office for a contravention of the provisions specified in clause (2) is disqualified from holding any other State office.
examples: That conduct is not acceptable when a State officer lies, abuses power, or behaves badly in office.
People expect leaders to act honestly and fairly. why that matters.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu maadili ya maafisa wa Serikali.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la maadili ya maafisa wa Serikali.

sections.title: Financial probity of State officers
sections.swTitle: Uaminifu wa kifedha wa maafisa wa Serikali
sections.simplified: State officers should handle money honestly.
sections.swSimplified: Maafisa wa Serikali inapaswa handle pesa kwa uaminifu.
sections.originalText:
(1) A gift or donation to a State officer on a public or official occasion is a gift or donation to the Republic and shall be delivered to the State unless exempted under an Act of Parliament.
(2) A State officer shall not— (a) maintain a bank account outside Kenya except in accordance with an Act of Parliament; or
(b) seek or accept a personal loan or benefit in circumstances that compromise the integrity of the State officer.
examples: State officers should handle money honestly and should not take secret gifts or benefits.
A leader uses office to get private financial favours. why that is wrong.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uaminifu wa kifedha wa maafisa wa Serikali.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la uaminifu wa kifedha wa maafisa wa Serikali.

sections.title: Restriction on activities of State officers
sections.swTitle: Vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali
sections.simplified: State officers cannot do everything they want while in office.
sections.swSimplified: Maafisa wa Serikali cannot do everything they want while in ofisi.
sections.originalText:
(1) A full-time State officer shall not participate in any other gainful employment.
(2) Any appointed State officer shall not hold office in a political party.
(3) A retired State officer who is receiving a pension from public funds shall not hold more than two concurrent remunerative positions as chairperson, director or employee of— (a) a company owned or controlled by the State; or
(b) a State organ.

(4) A retired State officer shall not receive remuneration from public funds other than as contemplated in clause (3).
examples: A State officer mixes public office with private business wrongly. there are limits.
A leader uses office for personal gain. why some activities are restricted.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali.

sections.title: Citizenship and leadership
sections.simplified: Some top leadership jobs require Kenyan citizenship rules to be followed.
sections.swSimplified: Some top viongozihip jobs require Mkenya uraia rules to be followed.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person is not eligible for election or appointment to a State office unless the person is a citizen of Kenya.
(2) A State officer or a member of the defence forces shall not hold dual citizenship.
(3) Clauses (1) and (2) do not apply to— (a) judges and members of commissions; or
(b) any person who has been made a citizen of another country by operation of that country’s law, without ability to opt out.
examples: Some top State jobs are only for Kenyan citizens.
A person holds another citizenship and wants a leadership office. why extra rules may apply.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uraia na uongozi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la uraia na uongozi.

sections.title: Legislation to establish the ethics and anti-corruption commission
sections.swTitle: Sheria ya kuanzisha tume ya maadili na kupambana na ufisadi
sections.simplified: This allows a body to fight corruption.
sections.swSimplified: Hii allows a body to fight corruption.
sections.originalText: Parliament shall enact legislation to establish an independent ethics and anti-corruption commission, which shall be and have the status and powers of a commission under Chapter Fifteen, for purposes of ensuring compliance with, and enforcement of, the provisions of this Chapter.
examples: Leaders steal or take bribes. why Kenya needs a body to fight corruption.
People want a public office to check dishonest leaders. that.
swExamples: Leaders steal or take bribes kwa nini Kenya needs a body to fight corruption.
Watu want a ofisi ya umma to kukagua dishonest viongozi. that.

sections.title: Legislation on leadership
sections.simplified: Parliament can make laws about leadership and integrity.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sheria kuhusu uongozi.
examples: Parliament can make those laws when Kenya needs stronger rules to keep leaders honest.
People want clear rules on leadership and integrity. the law can provide them.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu sheria kuhusu uongozi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la sheria kuhusu uongozi.

sections.title: General principles for the electoral system
sections.swTitle: Kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi
sections.simplified: Elections should be free and fair.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi.
examples: Elections should be free, fair, peaceful, and honest.
What should not happen when votes are stolen, people are threatened, or counting is unfair.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi.

sections.title: Legislation on elections
sections.simplified: Parliament can make laws to guide elections.
sections.swSimplified: Bunge inaweza kutunga sheria to guide uchaguzi.
examples: Parliament can make election laws to keep voting fair and orderly.
Kenya needs clearer election rules. Parliament can make them.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu sheria kuhusu uchaguzi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la sheria kuhusu uchaguzi.

sections.title: Registration as a voter
sections.simplified: This says who can register to vote.
sections.swSimplified: Hii says nani inaweza register to kura.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person qualifies for registration as a voter at elections or referenda if the person— (a) is an adult citizen;
(b) is not declared to be of unsound mind; and
(c) has not been convicted of an election offence during the preceding five years.

(2) A citizen who qualifies for registration as a voter shall be registered at only one registration centre.
(3) Administrative arrangements for the registration of voters and the conduct of elections shall be designed to facilitate, and shall not deny, an eligible citizen the right to vote or stand for election.
examples: An adult Kenyan citizen can register as a voter if the law allows it.
Someone is blocked from voter registration for no good legal reason. This Article can matter.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu registration as a kurar.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la registration as a kurar.

sections.title: Candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct
sections.swTitle: Candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct
sections.simplified: Candidates and parties must follow election rules.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct.
sections.originalText: In every election, all candidates and all political parties shall comply with the code of conduct prescribed by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission.
examples: Candidates and political parties must follow election rules and a code of conduct.
A candidate breaks campaign rules badly. why rules still matter.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct.

sections.title: Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate
sections.swTitle: Sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru
sections.simplified: You can run without a party, but rules still apply.
sections.swSimplified: You inaweza run without a party, but rules still apply.
sections.originalText:
Any person is eligible to stand as an independent candidate for election if the person— (a) is not a member of a registered political party and has not been a member for at least three months immediately before the date of the election; and
(b) satisfies the requirements of— (i) Article 99(1)(c)(i) or (ii), in the case of a candidate for election to the National Assembly or the Senate, respectively; or
(ii) Article 193(1)(c)(ii), in the case of a candidate for election to a county assembly.
examples: A person running without a political party must still follow the rules.
Someone says you can run with no rules at all. that is not true.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru.

sections.title: Voting
sections.simplified: Voting should be free, fair, and secret.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kupiga kura.
examples: Your vote should be secret, free, and counted fairly.
That is against the rules when people are forced, threatened, or cheated during voting.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kupiga kura.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la kupiga kura.

sections.title: Electoral disputes
sections.simplified: Election disputes should be settled fairly and by law.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu migogoro ya uchaguzi.
sections.originalText:
(1) Parliament shall enact legislation to establish mechanisms for timely settling of electoral disputes.
(2) Petitions concerning an election, other than a presidential election, shall be filed within twenty-eight days after the declaration of the election results by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission.
(3) Service of a petition may be direct or by advertisement in a newspaper with national circulation.
examples: Election disputes should be settled quickly and fairly.
People argue that an election was not fair. that the law must deal with it.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu migogoro ya uchaguzi.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la migogoro ya uchaguzi.

sections.title: Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission
sections.swTitle: Tume Huru ya Uchaguzi na Mipaka
sections.simplified: This sets up the body that runs elections and boundaries.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu tume Huru ya Uchaguzi na Mipaka.
examples: This office runs elections and handles boundaries work.
This office manages voting, voter lists, and some election boundaries.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu tume Huru ya Uchaguzi na Mipaka.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la tume Huru ya Uchaguzi na Mipaka.

sections.title: Delimitation of electoral units
sections.simplified: Voting area boundaries should be drawn fairly.
sections.swSimplified: Kupiga kura area boundaries inapaswa kuwa drawn kwa haki.
sections.originalText:
(1) There shall be two hundred and ninety constituencies for the purposes of the election of the members of the National Assembly provided for in Article 97(1)(a).
(2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall review the names and boundaries of constituencies at intervals of not less than eight years, and not more than twelve years, but any review shall be completed at least twelve months before a general election of members of Parliament.
(3) The Commission shall review the number, names and boundaries of wards periodically.
(4) If a general election is to be held within twelve months after the completion of a review by the Commission, the new boundaries shall not take effect for purposes of that election.
(5) The boundaries of each constituency shall be such that the number of inhabitants in the constituency is, as nearly as possible, equal to the population quota, but the number of inhabitants of a constituency may be greater or lesser than the population quota in the manner specified in clause (6) to take account of— (a) geographical features and urban centres;
(b) community of interest, historical, economic and cultural ties; and
(c) means of communication.

(6) The number of inhabitants of a constituency or ward may be greater or lesser than the population quota by a margin of not more than— (a) forty per cent for cities and sparsely populated areas; and
(b) thirty per cent for the other areas.

(7) In reviewing constituency and ward boundaries the Commission shall— (a) consult all interested parties; and
(b) progressively work towards ensuring that the number of inhabitants in each constituency and ward is, as nearly as possible, equal to the population quota.

(8) If necessary, the Commission shall alter the names and boundaries of constituencies, and the number, names and boundaries of wards.
(9) Subject to clauses (1), (2), (3) and (4), the names and details of the boundaries of constituencies and wards determined by the Commission shall be published in the Gazette, and shall come into effect on the dissolution of Parliament first following their publication.
(10) A person may apply to the High Court for review of a decision of the Commission made under this Article.
(11) An application for the review of a decision made under this Article shall be filed within thirty days of the publication of the decision in the Gazette and shall be heard and determined within three months of the date on which it is filed.
(12) For the purposes of this Article, "population quota" means the number obtained by dividing the number of inhabitants of Kenya by the number of constituencies or wards, as applicable, into which Kenya is divided under this Article.
examples: Voting areas should be drawn in a fair way.
One area is drawn unfairly to favour some people. why that is a problem.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu delimitation of electoral units.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la delimitation of electoral units.

sections.title: Allocation of party list seats
sections.simplified: Party list seats should be shared by clear rules.
sections.swSimplified: Party list seats inapaswa kuwa kugawanywa by clear rules.
sections.originalText:
(1) Elections for the seats in Parliament provided for under Articles 97(1)(c) and 98(1)(b), (c) and (d), and for the members of county assemblies under 177(1)(b) and (c), shall be on the basis of proportional representation by use of party lists.
(2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall be responsible for the conduct and supervision of elections for seats provided for under clause (1) and shall ensure that— (a) each political party participating in a general election nominates and submits a list of all the persons who would stand elected if the party were to be entitled to all the seats provided for under clause (1), within the time prescribed by national legislation;
(b) except in the case of the seats provided for under Article 98(1)(b), each party list comprises the appropriate number of qualified candidates and alternates between male and female candidates in the priority in which they are listed; and
(c) except in the case of county assembly seats, each party list reflects the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.

(3) The seats referred to in clause (1) shall be allocated to political parties in proportion to the total number of seats won by candidates of the political party at the general election.
examples: Party list seats should be shared by clear rules.
Special seats are being given out after an election. how that should be done.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu allocation of party list seats.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la allocation of party list seats.

sections.title: Basic requirements for political parties
sections.simplified: Political parties must follow democracy and national unity.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu basic requirements for political parties.
examples: Political parties should be democratic and should not divide Kenyans unfairly.
A party spreads hate or only serves one small group unfairly. why that is wrong.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu basic requirements for political parties.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la basic requirements for political parties.

sections.title: Legislation on political parties
sections.simplified: Parliament can make laws about political parties.
sections.swSimplified: Bunge inaweza kutunga sheria about political parties.
examples: Parliament can make more laws about political parties.
Kenya needs clearer party rules. Parliament can make them.
swExamples: Bunge inaweza kutunga more sheria about political parties.
Kenya needs clearer party rules. Bunge inaweza kutunga them.

sections.title: Establishment of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament has two Houses: the National Assembly and the Senate.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu establishment of Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) There is established a Parliament of Kenya, which shall consist of the National Assembly and the Senate.
(2) The National Assembly and the Senate shall perform their respective functions in accordance with this Constitution.
examples: Parliament has two Houses: the National Assembly and the Senate.
People ask how Parliament is set up. the basic shape.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu establishment of Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la establishment of Bunge.

sections.title: Role of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament makes laws and speaks for the people.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu role of Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) The legislative authority of the Republic is derived from the people and, at the national level, is vested in and exercised by Parliament.
(2) Parliament manifests the diversity of the nation, represents the will of the people, and exercises their sovereignty.
(3) Parliament may consider and pass amendments to this Constitution, and alter county boundaries as provided for in this Constitution.
(4) Parliament shall protect this Constitution and promote the democratic governance of the Republic.
(5) No person or body, other than Parliament, has the power to make provision having the force of law in Kenya except under authority conferred by this Constitution or by legislation.
(6) An Act of Parliament, or legislation of a county, that confers on any State organ, State officer or person the authority to make provision having the force of law in Kenya, as contemplated in clause (5), shall expressly specify the purpose and objectives for which that authority is conferred, the limits of the authority, the nature and scope of the law that may be made, and the principles and standards applicable to the law made under the authority.

Another office tries to make laws without Parliament. why that is not the normal path.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu role of Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la role of Bunge.

sections.title: Role of the National Assembly
sections.simplified: The National Assembly makes laws and watches over public money.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kazi ya Bunge la Taifa.
sections.originalText:
(1) The National Assembly represents the people of the constituencies and special interests in the National Assembly.
(2) The National Assembly deliberates on and resolves issues of concern to the people.
(3) The National Assembly enacts legislation in accordance with Part 4 of this Chapter.
(4) The National Assembly— (a) determines the allocation of national revenue between the levels of government, as provided in Part 4 of Chapter Twelve;
(b) appropriates funds for expenditure by the national government and other national State organs; and
(c) exercises oversight over national revenue and its expenditure.

(5) The National Assembly— (a) reviews the conduct in office of the President, the Deputy President and other State officers and initiates the process of removing them from office; and
(b) exercises oversight of State organs.

(6) The National Assembly approves declarations of war and extensions of states of emergency.
examples: The National Assembly makes laws and watches over public money.
The Assembly role when taxes, spending, or national government oversight are being discussed.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kazi ya Bunge la Taifa.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la kazi ya Bunge la Taifa.

sections.title: Role of the Senate
sections.simplified: The Senate protects counties and their interests.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kazi ya Seneti.
sections.originalText:
(1) The Senate represents the counties, and serves to protect the interests of the counties and their governments.
(2) The Senate participates in the law-making function of Parliament by considering, debating and approving Bills concerning counties, as provided in Articles 109 to 113.
(3) The Senate determines the allocation of national revenue among counties, as provided in Article 217, and exercises oversight over national revenue allocated to the county governments.
(4) The Senate participates in the oversight of State officers by considering and determining any resolution to remove the President or Deputy President from office in accordance with Article 145.
examples: The Senate protects counties and their interests.
County concerns are being discussed at national level. why the Senate matters.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kazi ya Seneti.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la kazi ya Seneti.

sections.swTitle: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as member of Parliament
sections.simplified: This says who can or cannot be elected to Parliament.
sections.swSimplified: Hii says nani inaweza or cannot be elected to Bunge.

sections.title: Election of members of Parliament
sections.simplified: This says how members of Parliament are elected.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu election of members of Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) A general election of members of Parliament shall be held on the second Tuesday in August in every fifth year.
(2) Whenever a vacancy occurs in the office of a member of the National Assembly under Article 97(1)(c), or of the Senate under Article 98(1) (b), (c) or (d), the respective Speaker shall, within twenty-one days of the occurrence of the vacancy, give notice in writing of the vacancy to— (a) the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission; and
(b) the political party on whose party list the member was elected or nominated.

(3) A vacancy referred to in clause (2) shall, subject to clause (5), be filled in the manner prescribed by an Act of Parliament within twenty-one days of the notification by the respective Speaker.
(4) Whenever a vacancy occurs in the office of a member of the National Assembly elected under Article 97(1)(a) or (b), or of the Senate elected under Article 98(1)(a)— (a) the respective Speaker shall, within twenty-one days after the occurrence of the vacancy, give notice in writing of the vacancy to the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission; and
(b) a by-election shall be held within ninety days of the occurrence of the vacancy, subject to clause (5).

(5) A vacancy referred to in clause (4) shall not be filled within the three months immediately before a general election.
examples: This Article says how members of Parliament are elected.
People ask when and how MPs are chosen. it.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu election of members of Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la election of members of Bunge.

sections.title: Term of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament serves for a set time.
sections.swSimplified: Bunge serves for a set time.
sections.originalText:
(1) The term of each House of Parliament expires on the date of the next general election.
(2) When Kenya is at war, Parliament may, by resolution supported in each House by at least two-thirds of all the members of the House, from time to time extend the term of Parliament by not more than six months at a time.
(3) The term of Parliament shall not be extended under clause (2) for a total of more than twelve months.
examples: Parliament stays for the period set here unless the law ends it earlier.
Parliament ends early in a way the law allows. that too.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu term of Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la term of Bunge.

sections.title: Vacation of office of member of Parliament
sections.swTitle: Vacation of ofisi of member of Bunge

sections.swSimplified: Hii says wakati an MP loses their seat.

sections.title: Right of recall
sections.simplified: Voters can remove an MP in some situations.
sections.swSimplified: Voters inaweza remove an MP katika hali fulani.
sections.originalText:
(1) The electorate under Articles 97 and 98 have the right to recall the member of Parliament representing their constituency before the end of the term of the relevant House of Parliament.
(2) Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for the grounds on which a member may be recalled and the procedure to be followed.

sections.title: Determination of questions of membership
sections.simplified: Courts decide disputes about whether someone is really an MP.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu determination of questions of membership.
sections.originalText:
(1) The High Court shall hear and determine any question whether— (a) a person has been validly elected as a member of Parliament; or
(b) the seat of a member has become vacant.

(2) A question under clause (1) shall be heard and determined within six months of the date of lodging the petition.
(3) Parliament shall enact legislation to give full effect to this Article.
examples: A court can decide that question when people argue about whether someone was properly elected as an MP.
There is a dispute about membership in Parliament. who helps settle it.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu determination of questions of membership.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la determination of questions of membership.

sections.swTitle: Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament

sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament.

People ask who leads debates in Parliament. it.

Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament.

sections.title: Presiding in Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament sittings must be led by the right person.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu presiding in Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) At any sitting of a House of Parliament— (a) the Speaker presides;
(b) in the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker presides; and
(c) in the absence of the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker, another member of the House elected by the House presides.

(2) At a joint sitting of the Houses of Parliament, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall preside, assisted by the Speaker of the Senate.
examples: Parliament must be led by the right person during a sitting.
The Speaker is away. who can take over the chair.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu presiding in Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la presiding in Bunge.

sections.title: Party leaders
sections.simplified: Party leaders have a place in Parliament.
sections.swSimplified: Party viongozi have a place in Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) There shall be a leader of the majority party and a leader of the minority party.
(2) The leader of the majority party shall be the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the largest party or coalition of parties.
(3) The leader of the minority party shall be the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the second largest party or coalition of parties.
(4) The following order of precedence shall be observed in the National Assembly— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly;
(b) the leader of the majority party; and
(c) the leader of the minority party.
examples: Party leaders have a formal place in Parliament.
Party leaders also matter in how Parliament works.
swExamples: Party viongozi have a formal place in Bunge.
Party viongozi also matter in jinsi gani Bunge hufanya kazi.

sections.title: Exercise of legislative powers
sections.simplified: Laws must follow the right steps.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu exercise of legislative mamlakas.
sections.originalText:
(1) Parliament shall exercise its legislative power through Bills passed by Parliament and assented to by the President.
(2) Any Bill may originate in the National Assembly.
(3) A Bill not concerning county government is considered only in the National Assembly, and passed in accordance with Article 122 and the Standing Orders of the Assembly.
(4) A Bill concerning county government may originate in the National Assembly or the Senate, and is passed in accordance with Articles 110 to 113, Articles 122 and 123 and the Standing Orders of the Houses.
(5) A Bill may be introduced by any member or committee of the relevant House of Parliament, but a money Bill may be introduced only in the National Assembly in accordance with Article 114.
examples: Parliament must use the steps the Constitution sets out when making a new law.
Someone tries to make a law by skipping the right process. that is not enough.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu exercise of legislative mamlakas.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la exercise of legislative mamlakas.

sections.title: Bills concerning county government
sections.simplified: Some Bills must be handled as county government Bills.
sections.swSimplified: Some Bills lazima iwe handled as serikali ya kaunti Bills.
sections.originalText:
(1) In this Constitution, "a Bill concerning county government" means— (a) a Bill containing provisions affecting the functions and powers of the county governments set out in the Fourth Schedule;
(b) a Bill relating to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; and
(c) a Bill referred to in Chapter Twelve affecting the finances of county governments.

(2) A Bill concerning county governments is— (a) a special Bill, which shall be considered under Article 111, if it— (i) relates to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; or
(ii) is the annual County Allocation of Revenue Bill referred to in Article 218; or

(b) an ordinary Bill, which shall be considered under Article 112, in any other case.

(3) Before either House considers a Bill, the Speakers of the National Assembly and Senate shall jointly resolve any question as to whether it is a Bill concerning counties and, if it is, whether it is a special or an ordinary Bill.
(4) When any Bill concerning county government has been passed by one House of Parliament, the Speaker of that House shall refer it to the Speaker of the other House.
(5) If both Houses pass the Bill in the same form, the Speaker of the House in which the Bill originated shall, within seven days, refer the Bill to the President for assent.
examples: Some Bills must be treated as county government Bills.
A new law affects counties. that it must follow the right path.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu bills concerning serikali ya kaunti.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la bills concerning serikali ya kaunti.

sections.title: Special Bills concerning county governments
sections.swTitle: Special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti
sections.simplified: This sets up county governments.
sections.swSimplified: Hii sets up serikali za kaunti.
sections.originalText:
(1) A special Bill concerning a county government shall proceed in the same manner as an ordinary Bill concerning county government, subject to clauses (2) and (3).
(2) The National Assembly may amend or veto a special Bill that has been passed by the Senate only by a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of the members of the Assembly.
(3) If a resolution in the National Assembly to amend or veto a special Bill fails to pass, the Speaker of the Assembly shall, within seven days, refer the Bill, in the form adopted by the Senate, to the President for assent.
examples: Some county Bills are special Bills and follow special rules.
A Bill about counties is especially important. why extra steps may apply.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti.

sections.title: Ordinary Bills concerning county governments
sections.swTitle: Ordinary Bills concerning serikali za kaunti
sections.simplified: This sets up county governments.
sections.swSimplified: Hii sets up serikali za kaunti.
sections.originalText:
(1) If one House passes an ordinary Bill concerning counties, and the second House— (a) rejects the Bill, it shall be referred to a mediation committee appointed under Article 113; or
(b) passes the Bill in an amended form, it shall be referred back to the originating House for reconsideration.

(2) If, after the originating House has reconsidered a Bill referred back to it under clause (1)(b), that House— (a) passes the Bill as amended, the Speaker of that House shall refer the Bill to the President within seven days for assent; or
(b) rejects the Bill as amended, the Bill shall be referred to a mediation committee under Article 113.
examples: Some county Bills are ordinary Bills and follow their own path.
A Bill affects counties but is not a special Bill. that route.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ordinary Bills concerning serikali za kaunti.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la ordinary Bills concerning serikali za kaunti.

sections.title: Mediation committees
sections.simplified: Mediation committees help the two Houses agree on a Bill.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu mediation committees.
sections.originalText:
(1) If a Bill is referred to a mediation committee under Article 112, the Speakers of both Houses shall appoint a mediation committee consisting of equal numbers of members of each House to attempt to develop a version of the Bill that both Houses will pass.
(2) If the mediation committee agrees on a version of the Bill, each House shall vote to approve or reject that version of the Bill.
(3) If both Houses approve the version of the Bill proposed by the mediation committee, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall refer the Bill to the President within seven days for assent.
(4) If the mediation committee fails to agree on a version of the Bill within thirty days, or if a version proposed by the committee is rejected by either House, the Bill is defeated.
examples: A mediation committee can help the two Houses try to agree on a Bill.
A law is stuck because the Houses do not agree. one way forward.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu mediation committees.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la mediation committees.

sections.title: Money Bills
sections.simplified: Money Bills deal with taxes, spending, and public money.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu money Bills.
examples: Money Bills deal with taxes, spending, and public money.
A Bill is mainly about raising or spending public money. that kind of Bill.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu money Bills.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la money Bills.

sections.title: Presidential assent and referral
sections.simplified: The President can sign a Bill or send it back.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu presidential assent and referral.
sections.originalText:
(1) Within fourteen days after receipt of a Bill, the President shall— (a) assent to the Bill; or
(b) refer the Bill back to Parliament for reconsideration by Parliament, noting any reservations that the President has concerning the Bill.

(2) If the President refers a Bill back for reconsideration, Parliament may, following the appropriate procedures under this Part— (a) amend the Bill in light of the President’s reservations; or
(b) pass the Bill a second time without amendment.

(3) If Parliament amends the Bill fully accommodating the President’s reservations, the appropriate Speaker shall re-submit it to the President for assent.
(4) Parliament, after considering the President’s reservations, may pass the Bill a second time, without amendment, or with amendments that do not fully accommodate the President’s reservations, by a vote supported— (a) by two-thirds of members of the National Assembly; and
(b) two-thirds of the delegations in the Senate, if it is a Bill that requires the approval of the Senate.

(5) If Parliament has passed a Bill under clause (4)— (a) the appropriate Speaker shall within seven days re-submit it to the President; and
(b) the President shall within seven days assent to the Bill.

(6) If the President does not assent to a Bill or refer it back within the period prescribed in clause (1), or assent to it under clause (5)(b), the Bill shall be taken to have been assented to on the expiry of that period.
examples: The President can sign it or send it back with concerns when Parliament passes a Bill.
This may explain why a Bill has not become law yet.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu presidential assent and referral.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la presidential assent and referral.

sections.title: Coming into force of laws
sections.simplified: A law starts working on the date set by the law.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu coming into force of sheria.
sections.originalText:
(1) A Bill passed by Parliament and assented to by the President shall be published in the Gazette as an Act of Parliament within seven days after-assent.
(2) Subject to clause (3), an Act of Parliament comes into force on the fourteenth day after its publication in the Gazette, unless the Act stipulates a different date on or time at which it will come into force.
(3) An Act of Parliament that confers a direct pecuniary interest on members of Parliament shall not come into force until after the next general election of members of Parliament.
(4) Clause (3) does not apply to an interest that members of Parliament have as members of the public.
examples: People need to know when a law starts working after Parliament passes it.
This Article explains when a new law starts working.
swExamples: Watu need to know wakati a sheria inaanza kufanya kazi after Bunge passes it.
Ibara hii inaeleza wakati a new sheria inaanza kufanya kazi.

sections.title: Powers, privileges and immunities
sections.simplified: Parliament needs freedom to debate and do its work.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu powers, privileges and immunities.
sections.originalText:
(1) There shall be freedom of speech and debate in Parliament.
(2) Parliament may, for the purpose of the orderly and effective discharge of the business of Parliament, provide for the powers, privileges and immunities of Parliament, its committees, the leader of the majority party, the leader of the minority party, the chairpersons of committees and members.
examples: MPs need protection while speaking or debating in Parliament so they can do their work properly.
Parliament members need certain powers and protections to check government well.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu powers, privileges and immunities.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la powers, privileges and immunities.

sections.title: Public access and participation
sections.simplified: Public participation.
sections.swSimplified: Public participation.
sections.originalText:
(1) Parliament shall— (a) conduct its business in an open manner, and its sittings and those of its committees shall be in public; and
(b) facilitate public participation and involvement in the legislative and other business of Parliament and its committees.

(2) Parliament may not exclude the public, or any media, from any sitting unless in exceptional circumstances the relevant Speaker has determined that there are justifiable reasons for the exclusion.
examples: The public should be able to follow and take part when Parliament discusses laws that affect people.
Leaders try to hide parliamentary work from citizens. openness and public participation matter.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu public access and participation.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la public access and participation.

sections.title: Right to petition Parliament
sections.simplified: People can take their concerns to Parliament.
sections.swSimplified: Watu inaweza take their concerns to Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has a right to petition Parliament to consider any matter within its authority, including to enact, amend or repeal any legislation.
(2) Parliament shall make provision for the procedure for the exercise of this right.
examples: People can take their concerns to Parliament by petition.
Citizens want Parliament to act on an issue. that they can petition it.
swExamples: Watu inaweza take their concerns to Bunge by petition.
Citizens want Bunge to act on an issue. that they inaweza petition it.

sections.title: Official languages of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament should use languages people can follow as the law provides.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu official languages of Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) The official languages of Parliament shall be Kiswahili, English and Kenyan Sign language, and the business of Parliament may be conducted in English, Kiswahili and Kenyan Sign language.
(2) In case of a conflict between different language versions of an Act of Parliament, the version signed by the President shall prevail.
examples: Parliament uses Kiswahili, English, and Kenyan Sign Language in its work.
People ask which languages Parliament should use. the basic rule.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu official languages of Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la official languages of Bunge.

sections.title: Quorum
sections.simplified: Parliament needs enough members present to do official business.
sections.swSimplified: Bunge needs enough members present to do official business.
sections.originalText:
The quorum of Parliament shall be– (a) fifty members, in the case of the National Assembly; or
(b) fifteen members, in the case of the Senate.
examples: Parliament needs enough members present to do official work.
Too few members are in the House. why business may have to stop.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu quorum.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la quorum.

sections.title: Voting in Parliament
sections.simplified: Voting should be free, fair, and secret.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kupiga kura in Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) Except as otherwise provided in this Constitution, any question proposed for decision in either House of Parliament shall be determined by a majority of the members in that House, present and voting.
(2) On a question proposed for decision in either House— (a) the Speaker has no vote; and
(b) in the case of a tie, the question is lost.

(3) A member shall not vote on any question in which the member has a pecuniary interest.
(4) In reckoning the number of members of a House of Parliament for any purpose of voting in that House, the Speaker of that House shall not be counted as a member.
examples: Members vote by rules when Parliament makes decisions.
There is a disagreement in Parliament. how a vote should be done.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kupiga kura in Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la kupiga kura in Bunge.

sections.title: Decisions of Senate
sections.simplified: The Senate follows voting rules when making decisions.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu decisions of Seneti.
sections.originalText:
(1) On election, all the members of the Senate who were registered as voters in a particular county shall collectively constitute a single delegation for purposes of clause (4) and the member elected under Article 98(1)(a) shall be the head of the delegation.
(2) When the Senate is to vote on any matter other than a Bill, the Speaker shall rule on whether the matter affects or does not affect counties.
(3) When the Senate votes on a matter that does not affect counties, each senator has one vote.
(4) Except as provided otherwise in this Constitution, in any matter in the Senate affecting counties— (a) each county delegation shall have one vote to be cast on behalf of the county by the head of the county delegation or, in the absence of the head of the delegation, by another member of the delegation designated by the head of the delegation;
(b) the person who votes on behalf of a delegation shall determine whether or not to vote in support of, or against, the matter, after consulting the other members of the delegation; and
(c) the matter is carried only if it is supported by a majority of all the delegations.
examples: The Senate follows voting rules when making decisions.
People ask how the Senate reaches a final decision. it.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu decisions of Seneti.

sections.title: Committees and Standing Orders
sections.simplified: Parliament works through committees and its own rules.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu committees and Standing Orders.
sections.originalText:
(1) Each House of Parliament may establish committees, and shall make Standing Orders for the orderly conduct of its proceedings, including the proceedings of its committees.
(2) Parliament may establish joint committees consisting of members of both Houses and may jointly regulate the procedure of those committees.
(3) The proceedings of either House are not invalid just because of— (a) a vacancy in its membership; or
(b) the presence or participation of any person not entitled to be present at, or to participate in, the proceedings of the House.

(4) When a House of Parliament considers any appointment for which its approval is required under this Constitution or an Act of Parliament— (a) the appointment shall be considered by a committee of the relevant House;
(b) the committee’s recommendation shall be tabled in the House for approval; and
(c) the proceedings of the committee and the House shall be in public.
examples: Parliament works through committees and its own rules.
A Bill or public issue is being studied closely. why committees matter.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu committees and Standing Orders.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la committees and Standing Orders.

sections.title: Power to call for evidence
sections.simplified: Parliament can call people and documents as evidence.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu power to call for evidence.
sections.originalText:
(1) Either House of Parliament, and any of its committees, has power to summon any person to appear before it for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information.
(2) For the purposes of clause (1), a House of Parliament and any of its committees has the same powers as the High Court— (a) to enforce the attendance of witnesses and examine them on oath, affirmation or otherwise;
(b) to compel the production of documents; and
(c) to issue a commission or request to examine witnesses abroad.
examples: Parliament can call people to answer questions and bring documents when checking a serious matter.
A committee needs proof before making a decision. it can ask for evidence.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu power to call for evidence.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la power to call for evidence.

sections.title: Location of sittings of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament usually sits in Nairobi unless the law allows otherwise.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu location of sittings of Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) A sitting of either House may be held at any place within Kenya and may commence at any time that the House appoints.
(2) Whenever a new House is elected, the President, by notice in the Gazette, shall appoint the place and date for the first sitting of the new House, which shall be not more than thirty days after the election.
examples: Parliament usually sits in Nairobi unless the law allows otherwise.

swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu location of sittings of Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la location of sittings of Bunge.

sections.title: Parliamentary Service Commission
sections.simplified: This sets up the office that helps Parliament work.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu tume ya Huduma za Bunge.
sections.originalText:
(1) There is established the Parliamentary Service Commission.
(2) The Commission consists of— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly, as chairperson;
(b) a vice-chairperson elected by the Commission from the members appointed under paragraph (c);
(c) seven members appointed by Parliament from among its members of whom— (i) four shall be nominated equally from both Houses by the party or coalition of parties forming the national government, of whom at least two shall be women; and
(ii) three shall be nominated by the parties not forming the national government, at least one of whom shall be nominated from each House and at least one of whom shall be a woman; and

(d) one man and one woman appointed by Parliament from among persons who are experienced in public affairs, but are not members of Parliament.

(3) The Clerk of the Senate shall be the Secretary to the Commission.
(4) A member of the Commission shall vacate office— (a) if the person is a member of Parliament— (i) at the end of the term of the House of which the person is a member; or
(ii) if the person ceases to be a member of Parliament; or

(b) if the person is an appointed member, on revocation of the person’s appointment by Parliament.

(5) Despite clause (4), when the term of a House of Parliament ends, a member of the Commission appointed under clause (2)(c) shall continue in office until a new member has been appointed in the member’s place by the next House.
(6) The Commission is responsible for— (a) providing services and facilities to ensure the efficient and effective functioning of Parliament;
(b) constituting offices in the parliamentary service, and appointing and supervising office holders;
(c) preparing annual estimates of expenditure of the parliamentary service and submitting them to the National Assembly for approval, and exercising budgetary control over the service;
(d) undertaking, singly or jointly with other relevant organisations, programmes to promote the ideals of parliamentary democracy; and
(e) performing other functions— (i) necessary for the well-being of the members and staff of Parliament; or
(ii) prescribed by national legislation.
examples: This office helps Parliament get the staff and offices it needs to do its work.
This office helps Parliament run day by day.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu tume ya Huduma za Bunge.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la tume ya Huduma za Bunge.

sections.title: Clerks and staff of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament has clerks and staff to help it work.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu clerks and staff of Parliament.
sections.originalText:
(1) There shall be a Clerk for each House of Parliament, appointed by the Parliamentary Service Commission with the approval of the relevant House.
(2) The offices of the Clerks and offices of members of the staff of the Clerks shall be offices in the Parliamentary Service.

sections.title: Principles of executive authority
sections.simplified: Top government power should be used to serve the people.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu principles of executive authority.
sections.originalText:
(1) Executive authority derives from the people of Kenya and shall be exercised in accordance with this Constitution.
(2) Executive authority shall be exercised in a manner compatible with the principle of service to the people of Kenya, and for their well-being and benefit.
examples: This shows how principles of executive authority works in the top national government.

swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu principles of executive authority.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la principles of executive authority.

sections.title: The National Executive
sections.simplified: The National Executive is the President, Deputy President, and Cabinet.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu the National Executive.
sections.originalText:
(1) The national executive of the Republic comprises the President, the Deputy President and the rest of the Cabinet.
(2) The composition of the national executive shall reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.
examples: This shows how the national executive works in the top national government.

sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu authority of the President.

sections.swTitle: Exercise of raisial mamlakas during temporary incumbency
sections.simplified: If the President is away, another leader acts for a while.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu exercise of raisial mamlakas during temporary incumbency.

sections.title: Decisions of the President
sections.simplified: Important presidential decisions must be in writing.
sections.swSimplified: Important raisial decisions lazima iwe in writing.
sections.originalText: A decision of the President in the performance of any function of the President under this Constitution shall be in writing and shall bear the seal and signature of the President.

A major presidential decision is only passed by word of mouth. why writing matters.

sections.title: Election of the President
sections.simplified: The President is chosen by voters in a national election.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu election of the President.
sections.originalText:
(1) The President shall be elected by registered voters in a national election conducted in accordance with this Constitution and any Act of Parliament regulating presidential elections.
(2) An election of the President shall be held— (a) on the same day as a general election of Members of Parliament, being the second Tuesday in August, in every fifth year; or
(b) in the circumstances contemplated in Article 146.
examples: The President is elected by voters in a national election.
People ask when a presidential election is held. the timing.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu election of the President.
Hii ni muhimu katika maisha ya kawaida unapokutana na suala la election of the President.

sections.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President
sections.swTitle: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President
sections.simplified: This says who can or cannot run for President.
sections.swSimplified: Hii says nani inaweza or cannot run for Rais.
examples: This Article says who can or cannot run for President.
A person wants to be President. the basic rules they must meet.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu qualifications and disqualifications for election as President.
